prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. With one income, two young children, and an owned home, this family should target **6–9 months of essential household expenses** in emergency savings. The exact dollar target depends on their actual monthly spending; race does not affect the calculation.

| Essential monthly expenses | 6-month fund | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $36,000 | $54,000 |

A reasonable planning estimate is **$24,000–$36,000** if essential expenses are about **$4,000 per month**. Use actual costs for property taxes, homeowners insurance, utilities, groceries, transportation, healthcare, childcare, minimum debt payments, and essential home repairs—excluding discretionary spending.

**Recommendation:** Build toward **nine months** rather than six because the household relies on one paycheck. Keep the money liquid in an FDIC-insured high-yield savings account. Also maintain a separate home-repair reserve so routine repairs do not consume the emergency fund.

I don’t have this family’s accounts or spending history connected, so this is a parameterized estimate rather than a personalized calculation.
